serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 200 g (one small bowl), as commonly served at Thai dessert stalls and hawker centres in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Tapioca with Coconut Milk — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Thai tapioca with coconut milk (cr_id F02050022). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No exact GI entry for Thai tapioca with coconut milk exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is assigned based on the closest analogous food: tapioca pearls (GI ~70–85 per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The lower bound of 70 is used conservatively. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 70 × (23.61 × 200/100) / 100 = 70 × 47.22 / 100 = 33.1, rounded to 32.9 using 200 g serving size and 23.61 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar and saturated fat from coconut milk in a low-potassium, low-fibre dessert context
hypertension.guidance: Sodium is low at 40 mg/100 g; however, the added sugar (8.2 g/100 g) and saturated fat from coconut milk independently elevate cardiovascular and blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines — limit portion size and frequency, and consider light coconut milk when preparing at home.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index tapioca pearls (GI ~70) with 23.6 g carbohydrate and 8.2 g sugar per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tapioca pearls have a very high GI (~70–85) and cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dessert, and if consumed, restrict to a very small portion (≤50 g) paired with a low-GI meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~1.8 g/100 g) and added sugar contributing to atherogenic dietary pattern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat from coconut milk and added sugar (8.2 g/100 g) contributes to an atherogenic dietary pattern; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption and prefer versions made with light coconut milk and reduced sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined tapioca starch and added sugar promote pro-inflammatory mediators; coconut milk saturated fat may contribute to low-grade inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tapioca is a refined starch with no anti-inflammatory properties; the added sugar and saturated fat from coconut milk may promote inflammatory mediators — individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consume this dessert only occasionally and in small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Tapioca with Coconut Milk (Thai: สาคูมะพร้าว, romanized: Saku Maprao) is a traditional Thai sweet dessert consisting of whole tapioca pearls cooked in sugar syrup until translucent and tender, then served with or submerged in sweetened coconut milk. The dish has a soft, chewy, slightly gelatinous texture from the tapioca pearls and a rich, mildly sweet, creamy flavour from the coconut milk. It is widely consumed in Thailand and is also popular in Singapore and other Southeast Asian countries as a hawker-stall and dessert-shop item. Per 100 g, the dish provides 116 kcal, 23.6 g carbohydrate, 8.2 g sugar, 2.1 g dietary fibre, 2.1 g fat (predominantly saturated from coconut milk), 0.6 g protein, and 40 mg sodium. The glycemic index of tapioca pearls is very high (GI ~70–85), making this dessert a high-glycemic-load food that causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation. It is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose. The saturated fat from coconut milk and added sugar also make it a moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal. It is classified as a processed dessert in the soup-dessert subgroup.
common_user_queries: Is Thai tapioca with coconut milk healthy?
How many calories in Thai tapioca dessert?

Is tapioca with coconut milk suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F02050022) with confidence 0.95; this is an approved primary regional source with an exact match for this dish. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved source (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008) and documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 1.80 g/100 g based on typical coconut milk contribution in this dish formulation; HPB source does not separately report saturated fat — this value is an approximation consistent with the fat total of 2.08 g/100 g and the known fatty acid profile of coconut milk. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this Thai tapioca with coconut milk preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — this is a plant-based dessert containing no animal-derived ingredients). Glycemic load per serving calculated using 200 g serving size: GL = 70 × (23.61 × 2.00) / 100 = 33.1 (reported as 32.9 using conservative rounding). Thai multilingual name สาคูมะพร้าว (Saku Maprao) is the standard Thai name for this dish.
